chunks.author: Alam, Naved et al.
chunks.title: Literary Maverick and Social Observer of Modern India.”
chunks.text:
1
“Exploring the Literary Contributions of Kushwant Singh: A 
Literary Maverick and Social Observer of Modern India.” 
Authors’:  
I. 
Naved Alam, ELT Researcher and Mass Communication Student, Aligarh Muslim University, 
II. 
Professor (Dr.) Md. Rizwan Khan, (Professor, Dept. of English, AMU) 
III. 
Faizur Rehman Sherwani, (MA English Literature Student, Dept. of English, AMU) 
IV. 
Dr. Rehan Khan (English Language Instructor & Former Assistant Prof. Dept. of English, AMU)
Abstract: 
This research paper aims to analyze the significant contributions of Kushwant Singh to Indian 
literature. Kushwant Singh, a prolific Indian writer, is celebrated for his thought-provoking 
novels, short stories, and journalistic works. His literary career spanned several decades, during 
which he explored a diverse range of themes, including history, politics, social issues, and 
human relationships. This paper will delve into Singh's unique writing style, his portrayal of 
characters, and the cultural and social contexts that influenced his work. Additionally, it will 
shed light on his impact on Indian literature and his enduring legacy as a distinguished author. 
Keywords: Kushwant Singh, Novelist, Journalist, Columnist, Partition of India, British prison in 
India, Indian literature. 
Background and Rationale 
Kushwant Singh, born on August 14, 1915, in Hadali, a small town now in Pakistan, was an 
acclaimed Indian author, journalist, and lawyer. He hailed from a Sikh family known for its 
military and administrative service to the British Raj. 
Singh completed his education at Government College, Lahore, and later pursued a law degree 
from King's College, London. He began his professional career as a lawyer in Lahore but soon 
transitioned to a career in journalism, working for several prominent publications, including The 
Illustrated Weekly of India, The National Herald, and The Hindustan Times.
2
As a writer, Kushwant Singh became renowned for his versatile literary contributions, spanning 
various genres. He published numerous novels, short stories, and collections of non-fiction 
essays. His literary works were characterized by their wit, satire, and candid exploration of 
controversial topics. 
Singh's literary career took off with the publication of his first novel, "The Mark of Vishnu and 
Other Stories," in 1950. He went on to write several acclaimed novels, including "Train to 
Pakistan" (1956), which explored the human tragedy and communal violence during the 
partition of India. The novel is considered one of his most significant works, highlighting his 
ability to intertwine historical events with compelling storytelling. 
In addition to his novels, Singh gained recognition for his collections of short stories, such as "I 
Shall Not Hear the Nightingale" (1959) and "The Portrait of a Lady" (1995). His short stories 
often delved into the complexities of human relationships, societal norms, and the challenges 
faced by individuals in the changing landscape of post-independence India. 
Apart from fiction, Kushwant Singh's non-fiction works, including "A History of the Sikhs" 
(1963), displayed his deep knowledge and research on Sikh history and culture. He also penned 
several volumes of autobiography, including "Truth, Love, and a Little Malice" (2002), where he 
candidly shared his personal experiences, encounters, and reflections. 
Kushwant Singh's writing style was marked by its simplicity, evocative descriptions, and the 
ability to tackle sensitive and taboo subjects fearlessly. His use of humor and satire added a 
distinctive flavor to his works, making them engaging and thought-provoking for readers. 
Throughout his career, Singh received numerous accolades and awards, including the Padma 
Vibhushan, India's second-highest civilian award, in 2007, for his contributions to literature and 
journalism. He was also honored with the Sahitya Akademi Fellowship, one of the highest 
literary honors in India. 
Kushwant Singh passed away on March 20, 2014, leaving behind a rich literary legacy that 
continues to inspire and captivate readers. His works remain relevant and serve as a testament
3
to his profound understanding of human nature, social dynamics, and the complexities of 
Indian society. 
Overview of his literary career 
Kushwant Singh had a prolific literary career that spanned several decades and encompassed a 
wide range of genres, including novels, short stories, essays, and non-fiction works. Throughout 
his career, he gained a reputation for his candid writing style, sharp wit, and fearless 
exploration of sensitive and controversial subjects. 
Singh's literary journey began with the publication of his first novel, "The Mark of Vishnu and 
Other Stories," in 1950. This collection of short stories showcased his ability to delve into the 
complexities of human relationships, societal norms, and the challenges faced by individuals in 
post-independence India. It laid the foundation for his distinctive storytelling style. 
One of Singh's most renowned and impactful works is the novel "Train to Pakistan" (1956). Set 
during the partition of India in 1947, the novel vividly depicts the violence and tragic events 
that occurred during that period. It is widely regarded as a powerful portrayal of the human 
tragedy and communal tensions that accompanied the partition. 
In addition to his novels, Singh published several collections of short stories that showcased his 
versatility as a writer. "I Shall Not Hear the Nightingale" (1959) and "The Portrait of a Lady" 
(1995) are among his notable works in this genre. His short stories often touched upon themes 
such as love, sexuality, societal norms, and the human condition, capturing the nuances of 
Indian society with honesty and humor. 
Apart from his fictional works, Singh made significant contributions to non-fiction as well. His 
extensive research and deep understanding of Sikh history and culture culminated in his 
monumental work, "A History of the Sikhs" (1963). This comprehensive book remains an 
essential reference for those interested in Sikhism and its historical context. 
Singh also delved into autobiography and memoir writing, sharing his personal experiences, 
reflections, and encounters in works like "Truth, Love, and a Little Malice" (2002) and "The
4
Sunset Club" (2010). These autobiographical works offered insights into Singh's own life while 
also reflecting the broader socio-political context of India. 
Throughout his literary career, Kushwant Singh's writing style remained consistent in its 
simplicity, directness, and humor. His works often tackled social, political, and cultural issues, 
challenging conventions and provoking thought. His ability to blend fiction and non-fiction, 
historical events and personal narratives, made his writing accessible and engaging to a wide 
range of readers. 
Kushwant Singh's contributions to literature were widely recognized and celebrated. He 
received numerous awards and honors, including the Sahitya Akademi Fellowship, the Padma 
Bhushan, and the Padma Vibhushan. His work continues to be studied, appreciated, and 
celebrated for its insightful portrayal of human nature, the complexities of Indian society, and 
the enduring legacy of historical events. In addition to his novels and short stories, Kushwant 
Singh was also a renowned journalist and columnist. He wrote for prominent Indian 
newspapers and magazines, including "The Illustrated Weekly of India" and "Hindustan Times." 
His columns were known for their wit, candidness, and sharp commentary on contemporary 
issues. 
Significance of his work in Indian literature: 
Kushwant Singh's work holds significant importance in the realm of Indian literature for several 
reasons. His contributions have left a lasting impact on readers, scholars, and the literary 
landscape of India. Here are some key aspects highlighting the significance of his work: 
I. 
Exploration of Historical Events: Singh's novels, particularly "Train to Pakistan," played a 
crucial role in shedding light on the human tragedy and communal violence during the 
partition of India. By capturing the emotional and psychological toll of this pivotal event 
in Indian history, he brought awareness and understanding to subsequent generations, 
contributing to the collective memory and historical consciousness of the nation. 
II. 
Candid Portrayal of Indian Society: Singh's writing fearlessly delved into the complexities 
and contradictions of Indian society. He openly discussed taboo subjects, societal

chunks.author: Alam, Naved et al.
chunks.title: Literary Maverick and Social Observer of Modern India.”
chunks.text:
5
norms, and cultural practices, challenging traditional beliefs and norms. His candid 
approach helped break societal barriers and fostered conversations on topics that were 
often considered off-limits, encouraging readers to reflect on and question prevailing 
ideologies. 
III. 
Exploration of Human Relationships: Singh's exploration of human relationships in his 
short stories and novels was marked by its realism and depth. He intricately depicted 
the complexities of love, friendship, and familial bonds, revealing the intricacies of 
human nature. His nuanced portrayals resonated with readers and offered insights into 
the intricacies of personal connections, further enriching the understanding of human 
emotions. 
IV. 
Blend of Humor and Satire: Singh's unique blend of humor and satire was a defining 
characteristic of his writing style. Through his wit and clever wordplay, he addressed 
serious social issues, political events, and cultural practices. This approach allowed him 
to engage readers while offering social commentary and critique, making his works both 
entertaining and thought-provoking. 
V. 
Representation of Punjabi Culture: As a Punjabi writer, Singh's works often celebrated 
and explored the rich cultural heritage of Punjab. His writings reflected the vibrant 
traditions, customs, and language of the region, contributing to the preservation and 
promotion of Punjabi culture in the larger Indian literary landscape. 
VI. 
Influence on Indian Literature: Singh's literary contributions have had a profound 
influence on subsequent generations of Indian writers. His bold and unapologetic 
approach to storytelling paved the way for new voices to emerge, encouraging 
experimentation and the exploration of diverse themes and writing styles. He inspired 
writers to challenge social norms, tackle sensitive subjects, and embrace a more 
authentic and inclusive narrative. 
VII. 
Public Intellectual and Cultural Critic: Beyond his literary works, Singh's role as a public 
intellectual and cultural critic also adds to his significance. Through his columns, essays, 
and interviews, he shared his thoughts on a wide range of topics, including politics,
6
religion, and social issues. His incisive commentary and fearless opinions made him a 
prominent voice in public discourse and contributed to shaping public opinion. 
Kushwant Singh's significance in Indian literature lies in his ability to capture the essence of 
Indian society, challenge conventions, and spark important conversations. His works continue 
to inspire and resonate with readers, making him a prominent figure in the literary canon of 
India. 
Writing Style and Themes:   
Kushwant Singh's writing style is characterized by its simplicity, directness, and engaging 
narrative. He had a knack for presenting complex ideas and situations in an accessible manner, 
making his works widely appealing to a diverse readership. His prose is clear, concise, and 
devoid of unnecessary ornamentation, which allows the reader to focus on the core themes 
and messages. 
Singh's use of humor and satire is a prominent aspect of his writing style. He employed wit and 
irony to comment on various social, political, and cultural aspects of Indian society. This blend 
of humor and satire added a distinctive flavor to his works, making them not only entertaining 
but also thought-provoking and incisive. 
The author was skilled at crafting well-rounded characters that felt authentic and relatable. He 
imbued them with human flaws and complexities, making them come alive on the pages of his 
novels and stories. His ability to capture the essence of human nature and emotions resonated 
deeply with readers, enhancing the overall impact of his storytelling. 
Themes:  
Kushwant Singh's literary works explored a wide array of themes, often touching upon 
significant aspects of Indian society and human experience. Some of the recurring themes in his 
writing include:
7
I. 
Partition and Communalism: As an eyewitness to the partition of India in 1947, Singh's 
works often grappled with the horrors and tragedies of that period. "Train to Pakistan" 
remains a seminal work in Indian literature that vividly portrays the communal violence 
and human suffering during the partition.
II. 
Human Relationships: Singh's stories delved into the complexities of human 
relationships, including love, friendship, family dynamics, and romantic entanglements. 
He skillfully explored the emotional intricacies and conflicts that arise within 
interpersonal connections.
III. 
Social Issues: His works fearlessly addressed social issues prevalent in Indian society, 
such as caste discrimination, gender inequality, religious tensions, and poverty. Through 
his storytelling, Singh aimed to highlight these issues, provoke discussion, and inspire 
positive change.
IV. 
Indian Culture and Traditions: Singh's Punjabi heritage often influenced his writing, and 
he celebrated Indian culture, traditions, and customs in many of his works. His stories 
were infused with the vibrant flavors of Indian festivals, rituals, and everyday life.
V. 
Political Satire: Singh was known for his scathing political satire, which he used to 
critique politicians, government policies, and the state of Indian politics. His writings 
often exposed the hypocrisy and corruption prevalent in the political landscape.
VI. 
Personal Reflections and Autobiography: Through his autobiographical works, Singh 
shared personal anecdotes, experiences, and reflections on his own life and encounters. 
This introspective aspect of his writing added depth and authenticity to his storytelling.
VII. 
Humanity and Compassion: Despite addressing challenging and at times dark subjects, 
Singh's works often carried a strong undercurrent of humanity and compassion. He
8
showcased the resilience of human spirit and the potential for empathy and 
understanding. 
Overall, Kushwant Singh's writing style and themes made him a prominent literary figure in 
India, as he managed to capture the essence of the nation's history, culture, and societal 
complexities with clarity and sensitivity. His works continue to be celebrated for their lasting 
impact on readers and their role in shaping Indian literature. 
Cultural and Social Contexts:  
Kushwant Singh's literary works are deeply rooted in the cultural and social contexts of India. 
His writings reflect the rich tapestry of the Indian society, its history, traditions, and the 
challenges it faced during his time. Several key cultural and social contexts are evident in his 
works: 
I. 
Partition of India: One of the most significant historical events that influenced Singh's 
writing was the partition of India in 1947. Being a witness to the communal violence and 
mass migrations during that period, Singh's novel "Train to Pakistan" serves as a 
poignant portrayal of the human tragedy and the devastating impact of partition on 
communities.
II. 
Diversity and Multiculturalism: India is a land of diverse cultures, languages, and 
religions. Singh's works often celebrate this multicultural fabric and present a nuanced 
portrayal of various communities coexisting in the Indian social landscape. He 
emphasized the importance of mutual respect and understanding among different 
religious and cultural groups.
III. 
Sikh Identity and Heritage: As a Sikh writer, Singh was deeply connected to his Punjabi 
heritage and the Sikh community. He frequently incorporated Sikh themes and 
characters in his works, and his historical research on Sikhism resulted in the acclaimed 
"A History of the Sikhs."
9
IV. 
Social Issues: Singh's writings fearlessly addressed prevailing social issues in Indian 
society. He touched upon topics such as caste discrimination, gender inequality, 
poverty, and communal tensions. By highlighting these issues, he sought to create 
awareness and encourage dialogue for social reform.
V. 
Colonial Legacy and Independence: Having grown up during the British colonial era and 
witnessing India's struggle for independence, Singh's works often carried the remnants 
of colonial influence and the euphoria and challenges of post-independence India.
VI. 
Changing Traditions and Values: Singh's stories often explore the clash between 
traditional values and modernity in Indian society. He reflected on how societal norms 
and cultural practices were evolving and adapting to the changing times.
VII. 
Political Context: Singh was an astute political observer, and his writings often engaged 
with the political climate of India. His political satires and commentaries provided 
critical insights into the state of Indian politics, governance, and the conduct of 
politicians.
VIII. 
Gender Dynamics: Singh's works also examined gender dynamics and women's roles in 
Indian society. He often portrayed strong female characters who challenged traditional 
gender roles and expectations.

chunks.author: Alam, Naved et al.
chunks.title: Literary Maverick and Social Observer of Modern India.”
chunks.text:
Through his literary explorations of these cultural and social contexts, Kushwant Singh 
contributed to a better understanding of India's historical journey, its diverse cultural identity, 
and the challenges faced by its people. His works continue to resonate with readers, providing 
valuable insights into the complex tapestry of Indian society. Moreover, they serve as a 
reminder of the nation's shared history and the importance of empathy, compassion, and 
mutual understanding in creating a harmonious society. 
Characterization and Human Relationships:
10
Kushwant Singh was a masterful storyteller known for his well-crafted characters and their 
intricate human relationships. His works often showcased the complexities of human nature 
and the dynamics that govern personal connections. Here are some key aspects of Singh's 
characterization and portrayal of human relationships in his literary works:
I. 
Multidimensional Characters: Singh's characters were vividly drawn, with a focus on 
their inner conflicts and complexities. Whether protagonists or supporting characters, 
they were portrayed as multi-faceted individuals with strengths, weaknesses, and 
personal dilemmas. This depth added authenticity to his stories, making the characters 
relatable and memorable.
II. 
Exploration of Emotions: Singh had a keen understanding of human emotions and was 
adept at portraying them in his characters. Love, fear, longing, joy, and sorrow were all 
delicately depicted, allowing readers to connect with the characters on an emotional 
level.
III. 
Realistic and Flawed Protagonists: Singh's protagonists were often flawed and faced 
with moral dilemmas. Their imperfections made them more human and relatable, as 
readers could empathize with their struggles and choices.
IV. 
Family and Friendships: Human relationships, particularly familial bonds and friendships, 
were a recurring theme in Singh's works. He depicted the complexities of family 
dynamics and explored the support, conflicts, and love shared between family 
members. Similarly, friendships were portrayed as sources of comfort and strength.
V. 
Romantic Relationships: Singh's novels often explored romantic relationships, delving 
into the intricacies of love and attraction. He portrayed the challenges and joys of 
romantic entanglements, giving readers a glimpse into the complexities of human love.
11
VI. 
Interpersonal Conflicts: Singh skillfully created situations that led to interpersonal 
conflicts, which revealed the true nature of his characters. These conflicts served as 
catalysts for character growth and development throughout the narratives.
VII. 
Generational Differences: His works also examined the contrasting worldviews of 
different generations. Through generational conflicts, he explored the changing values 
and perspectives in Indian society.
VIII. 
Cross-Cultural Relationships: Singh's works sometimes featured cross-cultural 
relationships, highlighting the challenges and beauty of connections formed across 
cultural divides. 
Overall, Kushwant Singh's characterization and portrayal of human relationships were a driving 
force behind the emotional impact of his stories. Through his nuanced understanding of human 
nature and interpersonal dynamics, he created narratives that continue to resonate with 
readers, offering insights into the complexities and universalities of the human experience. 
Impact on Indian Literature: 
Kushwant Singh's impact on Indian literature is profound and far-reaching. He left a lasting 
legacy as one of India's most celebrated and influential writers. Several aspects of his work and 
contributions have had a significant impact on Indian literature:
Exploration of Historical Events: Singh's powerful portrayal of the partition of India in his novel 
"Train to Pakistan" had a profound impact on Indian literature. His honest and poignant 
depiction of the human tragedy during the partition raised awareness of this critical event in 
the nation's history. It inspired other writers to delve into historical events and use fiction as a 
means of understanding and interpreting India's past.
I. 
Breaking Literary Taboos: Singh fearlessly addressed sensitive and controversial topics in 
his works, breaking literary taboos and challenging societal norms. By doing so, he
12
opened new avenues for Indian writers to explore a broader range of subjects, even 
those previously considered off-limits, and encouraged a more honest and candid 
approach to storytelling.
II. 
Satire as Social Commentary: Singh's use of humor and satire as a tool for social 
commentary became a defining feature of his work. His satirical writings on politics, 
religion, and society not only entertained readers but also provoked thought and 
introspection. His approach inspired other writers to employ satire as a means of 
critiquing the complexities of Indian society.
III. 
Influence on Contemporary Writers: Kushwant Singh's unique writing style and candid 
storytelling have been an inspiration to many contemporary writers. His ability to blend 
wit, humor, and sharp social commentary has influenced subsequent generations of 
Indian authors, who continue to experiment with similar techniques in their own works.
IV. 
Cultural Preservation and Promotion: Singh's works often celebrated Indian culture, 
traditions, and language, especially those of Punjab and the Sikh community. Through 
his writings, he contributed to the preservation and promotion of Indian cultural 
heritage, inspiring other writers to explore and celebrate diverse regional cultures.
V. 
Encouragement of Autobiographical Writing: Singh's candid autobiographical works 
inspired a trend of writers sharing their own life experiences and reflections. His 
openness in "Truth, Love, and a Little Malice" and other autobiographical works 
encouraged a new wave of autobiographical writing in Indian literature.
VI. 
Literary Journalism: Singh's career as a journalist also had an impact on Indian literature. 
His columns and essays demonstrated the power of literary journalism and its ability to 
engage readers with well-crafted prose and insightful commentary. This influenced
13
other journalists and writers to blur the lines between journalism and literature, 
resulting in a diverse range of literary non-fiction works.
VII. 
Widening the Audience for Indian Literature: Singh's engaging writing style and relatable 
characters attracted a wider audience, both within India and abroad. His works were 
translated into multiple languages, introducing Indian literature to global readers and 
enhancing its international recognition and appeal.
Kushwant Singh's impact on Indian literature goes beyond his writing. As a literary critic, he 
encouraged and promoted emerging talents, contributing to the growth of the Indian literary 
community. His contributions to literature, journalism, and social commentary have left an 
indelible mark on the literary landscape of India, making him an iconic figure in the nation's 
literary history.
Legacy and Contributions:
Kushwant Singh's legacy as a prominent Indian author and cultural icon is multifaceted and 
enduring. Kushwant Singh's contributions to literature are characterized by his candid 
storytelling, exploration of social issues, and celebration of Indian culture. His literary legacy 
continues to inspire and influence readers and writers alike, making him an iconic figure in 
Indian literature. His impact on the literary world remains enduring, and his works continue to 
be celebrated as valuable contributions to the literary canon.
His contributions to literature, journalism, and public discourse have left a lasting impact on 
Indian society and beyond. Here are some key aspects of his legacy and contributions:
I. 
Literary Legacy: Singh's literary works, spanning novels, short stories, essays, and non-
fiction, continue to be widely read and appreciated. His novels, particularly "Train to 
Pakistan," have become essential reading for understanding the partition of India. His
14
stories, filled with wit, satire, and human insights, remain relevant and engaging, 
appealing to readers across generations.
II. 
Fearless Writing: Singh's fearlessness in tackling controversial and sensitive subjects set 
him apart as a writer. He fearlessly addressed social issues, political events, and cultural 
taboos, contributing to a more open and honest literary landscape in India. His courage 
to speak his mind through his writing inspired other writers to be more daring in their 
own works.
III. 
Popularizing Indian Literature: Singh's engaging writing style and humorous storytelling 
attracted a broader audience to Indian literature. Through his works, he introduced 
Indian culture, history, and social realities to readers both within India and 
internationally, contributing to the popularity and recognition of Indian literature 
worldwide.
IV. 
Literary Journalism: As a journalist and editor, Singh's columns and editorial work 
showcased the power of literary journalism. He used his platform to offer insightful 
commentary on social and political issues, combining his literary prowess with 
journalistic integrity. His contributions to literary journalism have inspired many others 
to use their writing skills for insightful commentary on contemporary events.

chunks.author: Alam, Naved et al.
chunks.title: Literary Maverick and Social Observer of Modern India.”
chunks.text:
V. 
Encouraging New Writers: Throughout his career, Singh actively supported and 
encouraged emerging writers. He played a significant role in nurturing young talents and 
provided opportunities for them to showcase their work. His mentorship and guidance 
have had a positive impact on the growth of Indian literature.
VI. 
Cultural Criticism: Singh's critiques of Indian culture, politics, and society were both 
thought-provoking and influential. His candid observations on societal norms and
15
practices sparked public discourse and introspection, contributing to broader debates 
on pressing issues.
VII. 
Legacy as a Public Intellectual: Beyond his literary contributions, Singh was also known 
as a public intellectual and cultural critic. His engaging debates and lectures on diverse 
subjects enriched public discourse and influenced public opinion.
VIII. 
Bridging Communities: Singh's writings often bridged cultural divides and encouraged 
understanding between different communities in India. His portrayal of diverse 
characters and exploration of various cultural backgrounds fostered empathy and 
mutual respect among readers.
IX. 
Philanthropy: In addition to his literary pursuits, Singh also engaged in philanthropic 
activities. He donated royalties from some of his books to charitable causes, further 
solidifying his legacy as a socially responsible and compassionate individual.
Kushwant Singh's enduring legacy as a literary giant, cultural commentator and a champion of 
free expression continues to inspire and impact writers, readers, and thinkers around the 
world. His contributions to literature and public discourse have left an indelible mark on Indian 
society and cemented his position as one of the most influential and celebrated figures in 
Indian literature.
Conclusion: 
Kushwant Singh's literary career spanned several decades, during which he produced a diverse 
range of works, including novels, short stories, essays, and non-fiction and as a Punjabi writer, 
Singh celebrated Indian culture, traditions, and the diversity of the nation, contributing to the 
preservation and promotion of Indian cultural heritage.
16
"Train to Pakistan," one of his most renowned works, remains a powerful portrayal of the 
partition of India, shedding light on the human tragedy and communal violence during that 
period. Singh fearlessly addressed controversial and taboo subjects in his writing, challenging 
societal norms and promoting open dialogue on social issues and his works showcased well-
rounded characters and explored the complexities of human relationships, including love, 
friendship, and family bonds.
Kushwant Singh's work in literature continues to resonate with readers, leaving a lasting impact 
on Indian literature and the broader literary landscape. His legacy as an influential writer, 
cultural icon, and champion of free expression endures, inspiring writers and readers alike with 
his candid storytelling and social commentary. 
Kushwant Singh's literary journey is a testament to his profound impact on Indian literature and 
beyond. As a versatile writer, he fearlessly explored historical events, social issues, and human 
relationships, leaving behind a rich and diverse body of work. His candid portrayal of Indian 
society, with its complexities and contradictions, challenged conventional norms and paved the 
way for a more open and honest literary landscape.  
Singh's legacy lies not only in his compelling storytelling but also in his role as a public 
intellectual and cultural critic. Through his writings, he engaged readers in thought-provoking 
conversations about politics, culture, and societal norms. His use of humor, satire, and sharp 
commentary served as a powerful tool for social critique, inspiring subsequent generations of 
writers to follow suit.
The impact of Kushwant Singh's literature extends beyond the borders of India. His works, 
translated into numerous languages, have introduced Indian culture and history to a global 
audience, enhancing the country's literary recognition worldwide. Furthermore, Singh's support 
for emerging writers and his philanthropic contributions to charitable causes demonstrate his 
commitment to nurturing the literary community and making a positive impact on society.
17
As a writer, journalist, and cultural icon, Kushwant Singh's legacy continues to resonate with 
readers, scholars, and writers. His powerful storytelling, candid commentary, and celebration of 
Indian culture have left an indelible mark on Indian literature. His writings continue to inspire 
and provoke thought, making him an enduring figure in the literary canon and a cherished 
literary icon in the hearts of readers.

Longman, 1972. 
2. Pathak RS. Indian Fiction of the Nineties. Creative Books 1997.

4. Singh, Khushwant. A History of the Sikhs: Second Edition Oxford University Press, 1999;

chunks.text:
1
111 
 Rishika Sharma
VEDA’S 
JOURNAL OF ENGLISH LANGUAGE AND LITERATURE (JOELL) 
An International Peer Reviewed Journal 
http://www.joell.in
Vol.3 Issue 2 
2016 
RESEARCH ARTICLE
HISTORY AS LEIT- MOTIF IN KHUSHWANT SINGH’S TRAIN TO PAKISTAN 
Rishika Sharma 
 (P.hD Scholar,Dept. of English,University of Jammu)
ABSTRACT 
 Historical novel is the one that has a significant event of history at its 
backdrop. It is an established fact that literature and history move hand in 
hand. Literature gets affected by the historical movements and the writer 
cannot ignore the circumstances in which he is living or producing a work of art. 
In the history of India, partition has a very significant place. The partition and 
the subsequent developments formed a base for a whole range of writers from 
the subcontinent to express their feelings in the form of their works of art. 
Indian novelists like Khushwant Singh, Salman Rushdie, Manohar Malgonkar, 
Amitav Ghosh have described in detail about the horrors of partition. In this 
paper an attempt is made to analyze Train to Pakistan by Khushwant Singh. This 
novel was published in 1956 and it brings out the ordeals of partition and looks 
into the depressing past of Indian history. 
Keywords: History, Literature, India, Partition. 
Citation: 
APA Sharma,R. (2016) History as Leit- Motif in Khushwant Singh’s Train to Pakistan.Veda’s 
 Journal of English Language and Literature-JOELL, 3(2), 111-113. 
MLA Sharma,Rishika “History as Leit- Motif in Khushwant Singh’s Train to Pakistan.Veda’s Journal of 
 English Language and Literature-JOELL 3.2(2016):111-113.
© Copyright VEDA Publication 
Khushwant Singh’s Train to Pakistan was 
published originally with the title Mano Majra and is 
considered one of the best realistic novels of Indian 
English fiction. It is the first comprehensive study of 
partition and its drastic impact on the individuals of a 
village. It brings out how partition breaks down a 
communally hormonal nation. Khushwant Singh had 
the first-hand experience of partition and this is the 
reason why he was so realistic in his portrayal of the 
same. C.N. Srinath labels him as “the witness turned 
writer” (59) and N.S. Gundur opines, “that he 
happened to write Train to Pakistan as a therapeutic 
exercise to overcome the pangs of partition” (76). 
The novel is set in 1947 when the weather 
was much hotter and much drier than usual but in 
Mano Majra all was well as the partition affected the 
elites immediately whereas the villages and the 
common people living in rural areas were hardly 
aware of it unless and until the shadow of partition 
started haunting their peaceful and communal
2
112 
 Rishika Sharma
VEDA’S 
JOURNAL OF ENGLISH LANGUAGE AND LITERATURE (JOELL) 
An International Peer Reviewed Journal 
http://www.joell.in
Vol.3 Issue 2 
2016 
environment. Indian subcontinent that was governed 
by British was divided in 1947. The idea for a 
separate Muslim state was conceptualized by a poet 
and political thinker Mohd. Iqbal in 1930 and this 
idea for a separate homeland for Indian Muslims to 
be called Pakistan took a definite shape in the mind 
of Chaudhary Rehmat Ali and the proponent of a two 
nation theory Mohd. Ali Jinnah, was too obsessed to 
have a separate Muslim state that he overlooked the 
disastrous consequences of the same. N.S. Gundur in 
the introduction of his book Partition and Indian 
English Fiction writes, 
“The emergence of these two nations at 
the end of colonial rule, celebrated as the 
crowning 
achievement 
of 
the 
freedom 
movement, was nevertheless accompanied by 
horrendous 
tragic 
events 
because 
of 
the 
unplanned way the leaders proceeded to 
partition of the country” (1). 
The partition of a mighty nation was done 
and the aftermaths were being looked at in the state 
of sheer shock by political as well as the general 
public. 
Riots 
and 
robberies 
were 
frequent, 
accompanied by mass murders and rapes. S. Settar 
and Indira Gupta in this context writes, “One will 
never know exactly how many people died in the 
riots which accompanied it, but around seventeen 
million people had to relocate themselves across the 
newly drawn boundaries” (7). 
Partition was the most horrible phase of 
Indian history. In one go it disturbed the communal 
harmony being shared by the people of subcontinent 
since ages. Not only a physical division, it was more 
of a psychological, moral and social division. As the 
abstract puts that history and art move hands in hand 
so there are a number of writers, painter 
cinematographers who accounted for partition in 
their own style and art form. A work of fiction has a 
story / plot with a central character and his life. It 
traces the problem with the life of an individual in 
relation to the society in which he/she is living. 
History talks of a specific time, date and event 
whereas a work of fiction has a character who is 
struggling at a particular time and event so it is 
worth quoting N.S.Gundur who says,“However it 
doesn’t mean that fictional narratives neglect politico 
– historical aspect. They portray both the human and 
political problems. Sometimes they act as critiques of 
the political history and gives insights into the reality 
of politics of partition” (4). 
By reading books on history of partition one 
may come to know about the event and its 
aftermaths but by reading a fictional work dealing 
with history one understands the whole process and 
its impacts upon individuals and society on the 
whole. Mano Majra stands symbolic for India and a 
very authentic reaction has been portrayed by 
Khushwant Singh. A village equally populated with 
Sikhs and Muslims was unaware about the 
aftermaths of partition unless and until a train loaded 
with dead bodies of Hindus and Sikhs arrived at the 
station. 
The trains were symbolic of life’s movement 
in Mano Majra. They were like the timetable for the 
villagers and it was part of their routine to hear the 
trains’ chugging around every day. They had their 
routine set upon the time of the trains that used to 
pass from the station but after the arrival of the train 
loaded with dead bodies there were very less trains 
for the route which resulted in a lack of sense of time 
for the people of Mano Majra. “People stayed in bed 
late without realizing that times had changed and the 
mail train might not run through at all. Children did 
not know when to be hungry and clamored for food 
all the time . . . Goods trains had stopped running all 
together so there was no lullaby to lull them to sleep. 
Instead, ghost trains went past at odd hours between 
midnight and dawn disturbing the dreams of Mano 
Majra” (93). 
The train that halted at the Mano Majra 
station changed the entire scenario of the village and 
the villagers as well. Before the arrival of this train of 
the dead there used to be communal harmony in the 
village. A local deity called ‘Doe’ was worshiped by all 
the residents of Mano Majra. Suja Alexander writes, 
“The peace loving people of Mano Majra are injected 
with the venom of communalism and egoism by the 
people in the power only to further their own nest. 
They succeeded in divorcing the people from their 
much – loved brethren, which results in alienation, 
enmity, hatred, anger and revenge” (45). 
Apart from partition another story finds its 
place in the novel. It is the love story of Jugga the 
badmash and Nooran a Muslim girl.As the story
3
113 
 Rishika Sharma
VEDA’S 
JOURNAL OF ENGLISH LANGUAGE AND LITERATURE (JOELL) 
An International Peer Reviewed Journal 
http://www.joell.in

chunks.author: Sharma, Rishika
chunks.title: An International Peer Reviewed Journal
chunks.journal: Journal of English Language and Literature-JOELL, 3(2), 111-113
chunks.text:
Vol.3 Issue 2 
2016 
moves forward we come to know how partition 
affected the lives of these two lovers.Train to 
Pakistan is divided into 4 sections: 
a. Dacoity 
b. Kaliyug 
c. 
Mano Majra 
d. Karma 
The story starts with the dacoity and the 
murder of Lala Ram Lal the only Hindu resident of 
Mano Majra. The district commissioner Hukum 
Chand orders the arrest of Jugga and Iqbal Singh who 
came to Mano Majra on the very day of dacoity. Both 
the arrested persons were innocent as the crime was 
committed by Malli and his gang and they hatched a 
plan against Jugga by scattering broken bangles in 
front of his house. After the dacoity it is the Kaliyug 
that is being projected before the readers. As is 
stated in the Hindu religious texts that Kaliyug will be 
the last and the most sinful period of the history of 
earth and so readers witness bloodshed, mass 
murders, rapes and many more human incidents in 
the due course. The trains loaded with dead bodies 
were brutally exchanged from both sides of the 
border. Mano Majra also got involved in the same. 
When the ghostly train arrived in Mano Majra 
strange things were observed in the village. The 
villagers were asked by the soldiers to arrange as 
much wood and kerosene as they can. The villagers 
were not clear about what all was going on but later 
when the smoke and smell of charred bodies spread 
over the village, the people were horrified. Not only 
the general masses but the police officers like the 
sub-inspector also wanted to take revenge on the 
Muslims living there but Hukum Chand maintains his 
beaurocratic temperament and orders safe exit for 
Muslims to Pakistan. While the Muslim community 
was being evacuated, the dacoits like Malli and his 
gang were taking charge of the homes and the 
livestock of Muslims families. The village was entirely 
chaotic. River Satluj was flowing with the dead 
remains and a perfect picture of kalyug has been 
portrayed by the author. 
Kalyug manifests itself in Mano Majra, a 
place so peaceful and hardly aware of anything like 
partition, and it gets involved in the acts of 
inhumanity. The elders of the village wanted to 
provide shelter to their muslim friends but the 
muslims were advised by the administration to leave 
for Pakistan as soon as possible. Thus, Mano Majra 
has been projected as a place with both good and evil 
characteristics. 
The last part that is the Karma forms the 
climax of the novel. Hukum Chand releases both 
Juggat Singh and Iqbal Singh with the intention that 
they might be able to save the lives of the muslims 
who were to leave Chundunnugger for Pakistan. Iqbal 
Singh took refuge in a gurudwara but Juggat when 
realized that his love Nooranwould also be going, 
decides to let go the train safely to the other side of 
border. He was totally in to the theory of karma and 
was hardly aware of the results. He bravely cut the 
rope which was meant to stop the train. 
“He went at it with his knife, and then with 
his teeth. The engine was almost on him. There was a 
volley of shots. The man shivered and collapsed. The 
rope snapped at the center as he fell. The train went 
over him and went on to Pakistan” (208). Thus to 
quote P.K. Singh “the devotional love of Jugga to 
Nooran, and Hukum Chand to Hasseena, finally won 
over the communal feelings dividing people from one 
another” (56). 
After analyzing Train to Pakistan we come 
across so many events and incidents that were 
accompanied by the partition of India. A very 
authentic and dependable portrayal of the same has 
been done by the author. His experience being first-
hand, has been the source of inspiration for him. This 
dark event of the Indian history affected many lives 
and through this novel one comes to know the perils 
of 
partition 
in 
a 
more 
comprehensive 
and 
commendable manner. 
REFERENCES 
[1]. Gundur, N.S. Partition and Indian English Fiction. New 
Delhi: Adhyayan, 2008. Print. 
[2]. Indira Bhatt eds. The Fictional World of Khushwant 
Singh. New Delhi: Creative Books, 2001. Print. 
[3]. Settar, S. and Indira Baptista Gupta, Introduction. Pangs 
of Partition. Vol. 1. New Delhi: Manohar, 2002. Print. 
[4]. Singh, Khushwant. Train to Pakistan. New Delhi: Ravi 
Dayal, 1988. Print. 
[5]. Singh, P.K. The Novels of Khushwant Singh: A Critical 
Evaluation. Jaipur: Book Enclave, 2005. Print. 
[6]. Srinath, C.N. “The Writer as Historical Witness: 
Khushwant Singh’s Train to Pakistan and Chaman 
Nahal’s Azadi.” The Literary Criterion. 25. 2 (1990) Print.

chunks.author: Goel, Manjula
chunks.title: Analysis of Train to Pakistan, Film Adaptation
chunks.journal: Journal of Novel Research and Development (www
chunks.text:
1
© 2022 IJNRD | Volume 7, Issue 1 January 2022 | ISSN: 2456-4184 | IJNRD.ORG
IJNRD2201026 
International Journal of Novel Research and Development (www.ijnrd.org)
202 
Analysis of Train to Pakistan, Film Adaptation 
from Khushwant Singh's Novel: Similarities and 
Differences 
Dr Manjula Goel 
Associate Professor 
Department of English 
R.K.S.D.(P.G.) College Kaithal India
Abstract 
The film Train to Pakistan (1998), directed by Pamela Rooks, is a cinematic adaptation of Khushwant Singh's 
1956 novel of the same name. The story is set against the backdrop of the Partition of India in 1947, depicting 
the devastation and turmoil faced by individuals during this period of mass migration, violence, and 
dehumanization. As V.A. Shahane opines, “It is a grim story of individuals and communities caught into the 
vortex of partition of undivided India into two states in 1947” (Shahane 332). While Rooks’ adaptation 
remains largely faithful to the core themes and narrative of the novel, the film inevitably condenses, 
streamlines, and emphasizes certain aspects for cinematic impact. This analysis explores both the similarities 
and differences between the novel and its film adaptation. 
Key Words: film adaptation, dehumanization, partition, religion 
________________________________________________________________________________________________________ 
Introduction: As a reflection of reality, the films attract a wider range of audience than literature as they 
transcend barriers of religion, class and language. However, Literature serves a significant source of material 
for cinematic adaptations. The Visualisation of literary scenes captivates viewers through its rich portrayal of 
imagery, music and emotions. The study examines the film adaptation of Khushwant Singh’s Train to 
Pakistan. The author was born in Haladi, now in Pakistsan. He was the most learned and versatile personality. 
Train to Pakistan was his debut novel. M.R. Anand, RK Narayan. Raja Rao were his inspirations. He was 
awarded Padma Bhushan, Padma Vibhushan, Groove Press Award and Honest Man of the year Award. He 
gained recognition as lawyer, journalist, ambassador, and a literary writer.
2
© 2022 IJNRD | Volume 7, Issue 1 January 2022 | ISSN: 2456-4184 | IJNRD.ORG
IJNRD2201026 
International Journal of Novel Research and Development (www.ijnrd.org)
203 
Similarities Between the Novel and the Film 
1. Central Theme and Setting: Both the novel and the film are set in the village of Mano Majra, located on the 
Indo-Pakistan border. The community, initially depicted as living in harmony, is eventually torn apart by the forces of 
Partition. The Sikh and Muslim villagers, who had coexisted peacefully, are thrust into violence and mistrust. The 
arrival of the “ghost train,” which carries the bodies of massacred people, becomes a pivotal symbol of the communal 
violence that disrupts this idyllic life. 
2. Main Characters: The central characters in both the book and film remain the same, with Jugga Singh, a Sikh 
“badmash” (outlaw), and Nooran, a Muslim girl, at the heart of the story. Their love affair, which transcends religious 
and communal boundaries, reflects the human cost of the Partition. Additionally, the characters of Iqbal, a progressive 
socialist, and Hukumchand, the District Commissioner, play essential roles in the narrative. 
3. Key Events: Several key events from the novel are preserved in the film. These include the murder of Lala 
Ramlal, the dacoity committed by Mali and his gang, and the subsequent framing of Jugga and Iqbal for the crime. The 
film also captures the arrival of the ghost train, the escalating tension between Muslims and Sikhs, and Jugga’s tragic 
sacrifice to prevent the train from being blown up, saving Nooran and others in the process. 
4. Symbolism of the Train: The train in both the novel and the film serves as a powerful symbol. Initially 
representing British Imperialism, it gradually transforms into a symbol of the carnage and tragedy caused by the 
Partition. The arrival of the ghost train, carrying massacred bodies, becomes an emblem of the bloodshed and loss that 
defined this period in history. 
5. Realistic Portrayal of Violence: The depiction of violence in the film, particularly the arrival of the mutilated 
bodies of massacred Sikhs and Hindus, closely follows the descriptions in the novel. Rooks emphasizes the brutal 
realities of Partition, with scenes such as the close-up of Imam Baksh’s fearful expression as he anticipates the train, 
which captures the horror and helplessness of the situation. 
Differences Between the Novel and the Film 
1. Condensed Timeline: The novel is more expansive in its treatment of time and characters. It delves deeply into 
the backstories and motivations of the characters, particularly Jugga and Iqbal. The film, however, condenses the 
timeline, focusing more on the central conflict and the immediate sense of urgency and tension surrounding the Partition. 
This condensation inevitably leads to the loss of certain character nuances and subplots, including Iqbal’s political 
ideology and Jugga’s past.
3
© 2022 IJNRD | Volume 7, Issue 1 January 2022 | ISSN: 2456-4184 | IJNRD.ORG
IJNRD2201026 
International Journal of Novel Research and Development (www.ijnrd.org)
204 
2. Character Development: While the novel provides a rich exploration of character motivations, the film 
simplifies some of these aspects for brevity. For example, Iqbal’s political stance as a socialist is more subtly introduced 
in the film, and his internal conflict regarding the violence of Partition is not as thoroughly explored as in the novel. He 
is a cynical intellectual who questions the efficacy of political solutions and grapples with the limitations of his own 
idealism. The film presents him as a more straightforward idealist, driven by a desire to bring about social change. 
Similarly, Hukumchand’s character is more complex in the novel, where he grapples with his own moral dilemmas and 
the futility of his position. He is torn between his duty to maintain order and his growing awareness of the injustice 
being inflicted upon the Muslim population. whereas the film simplifies his character somewhat, focusing more on his 
role as a representative of authority and portrays him as more of a helpless bureaucrat. However, there are subtle 
differences in the portrayal of juggat Singh and Nooran. These adaptations in character portrayal reflect the constraints 
of the cinematic medium and the need to condense complex narratives for the screen. 
3. Streamlining of Subplots: The film adapts the central love story between Jugga and Nooran with greater 
emphasis, while sidelining some of the secondary characters and subplots present in the novel. For instance, the role of 
the teenage prostitute Haseena, who has an affair with Hukumchand in the novel, is minimized in the film, as is the 
deeper exploration of the moral ambiguities faced by the various characters in the village. 
4. Focus on Action and Tension: The film places greater emphasis on the suspense and action surrounding the 
climax, where Jugga attempts to stop the train and save Nooran. This shift focuses on the physical and emotional 
intensity of the final moments, whereas the novel builds up to this conclusion more slowly, allowing the reader to reflect 
on the larger political and social implications of the events. The film, due to its medium, must focus on the immediacy 
of the events, whereas the novel provides more space for introspection and commentary on the Partition.  
5. Use of Music: The song “Sanu Aa Milya Yaar Piyaariya,” composed by Sai Bulleshah, plays a significant role 
in the film, setting the tone for the peaceful existence of the villagers before the chaos of Partition. This song, with its 
evocative lyrics and imagery, serves as a subtle narrative device to underscore the harmony that once existed in Mano- 
Majra. The novel, however, lacks this musical element and instead focuses more on descriptive passages to capture the 
essence of village life. 
Conclusion -The film Train to Pakistan is a sincere attempt to bring Khushwant Singh’s powerful narrative to the 
screen and remains largely faithful to the novel in terms of its central themes, characters, and key events, it necessarily 
condenses and streamlines certain aspects for cinematic purposes. Rooks succeeds in capturing the emotional intensity 
of the story, emphasizing the personal sacrifices made against the backdrop of national tragedy. However, the deeper, 
more complex reflections on Partition present in Singh’s novel are somewhat diminished in the film, as it focuses more

chunks.author: Goel, Manjula
chunks.title: Analysis of Train to Pakistan, Film Adaptation
chunks.journal: Journal of Novel Research and Development (www
chunks.text:
4
© 2022 IJNRD | Volume 7, Issue 1 January 2022 | ISSN: 2456-4184 | IJNRD.ORG
IJNRD2201026 
International Journal of Novel Research and Development (www.ijnrd.org)
205 
on the immediate emotional impact of the events. Overall, Train to Pakistan the film is a poignant retelling of a tragic 
chapter in Indian history, staying true to the novel’s core message while adapting it for the visual medium. 
References:  
 
Aziz, K.K. History of Partition of India. New Delhi:  Atlantic Publishers & Distributors 1995, (1-4)  
 
Bhatnagar, Manmohan.K. Modern Indian English Novels. New Delhi: Atlantic, 2003. 
 
Dhawan, R.K. (ed.) Khushwant Singh – The Man and the Writer, New Delhi: Prestige Books, 2001. 
 
Nahal, Chaman. Telling of Time Past. The Hindustan Times weekly. March 15, 1981. 
 
Singh, J. Jinnah: India-Partition-Independence. New Delhi: Rupa& Co. 2009. 
 
Singh, K. I Shall not see the Nightingale. New Delhi: Ravi Dayal. 1988. 
 
Singh, K. Train to Pakistan. New Delhi: Ravi Dayal. 1988. 
 
Singh, Khushwant. Delhi. New Delhi: Penguin Books India Ltd. 1990.  
 
Singh, Khushwant Need for a New Religion in India & Other Essay. New Delhi: UBS  
Publishers 1991.

5. Use of Music: The song “Sanu Aa Milya Yaar Piyaariya,” composed by Sai Bulleshah, plays a significant role 
in the film, setting the tone for the peaceful existence of the villagers before the chaos of Partition. This song, with its 
evocative lyrics and imagery, serves as a subtle narrative device to underscore the harmony that once existed in Mano- 
Majra. The novel, however, lacks this musical element and instead focuses more on descriptive passages to capture the 
essence of village life. 
Conclusion -The film Train to Pakistan is a sincere attempt to bring Khushwant Singh’s powerful narrative to the 
screen and remains largely faithful to the novel in terms of its central themes, characters, and key events, it necessarily 
condenses and streamlines certain aspects for cinematic purposes. Rooks succeeds in capturing the emotional intensity 
of the story, emphasizing the personal sacrifices made against the backdrop of national tragedy. However, the deeper, 
more complex reflections on Partition present in Singh’s novel are somewhat diminished in the film, as it focuses more

chunks.author: Goel, Manjula
chunks.title: Analysis of Train to Pakistan, Film Adaptation
chunks.journal: Journal of Novel Research and Development (www

International Journal of Novel Research and Development (www.ijnrd.org)
205 
on the immediate emotional impact of the events. Overall, Train to Pakistan the film is a poignant retelling of a tragic 
chapter in Indian history, staying true to the novel’s core message while adapting it for the visual medium.

Citation: Saeed, B., Shafi, S., & Masood, M. H. (2023). Exploring Identity and 
Belonging in the Context of Partition of 1947: A Social Identity Theory Analysis of 
‘Train to Pakistan’ by Khushwant Singh. Global Language Review, VIII(I), 204-215. 
https://doi.org/10.31703/glr.2023(VIII-I).20
§ Vol. VIII, No. I (Winter 2023) 
§ Pages: 204 ‒ 215 
§ DOI: 10.31703/glr.2023(VIII-I).20
Exploring Identity and Belonging in the Context of 
Partition of 1947: A Social Identity Theory Analysis of 
‘Train to Pakistan’ by Khushwant Singh
Bushra Saeed 
M.Phil Scholar, Department of English Literature, Fatima Jinnah 
Women University, Rawalpindi, Punjab, Pakistan. 
Shahzeb Shafi 
M.Phil. Scholar, Faculty of Arts and Humanities, National 
University of Modern Languages, Islamabad, Pakistan. 
Muhammad 
Hamzah Masood 
Lecturer, Department of Linguistics and Communications, 
University of Management and Technology, Lahore, Punjab, 
Pakistan.
Abstract: This study explores the theme of identity and belonging in Khushwant Singh’s novel, ‘Train 
to Pakistan’, by investigating how the characters develop their sense of identity and belonging within 
the socio-political context of the Partition of India in 1947. The study employs a qualitative research 
methodology, analyzing the novel through close reading and thematic analysis. The study applies the 
Social Identity Theory to examine the novel’s categorization, social comparison, and identification 
processes. The research reveals that religious, cultural, and national affiliations significantly influence 
the characters’ identities and sense of belonging. It highlights the complexities of identity construction 
during conflict and demonstrates that characters’ identities are subject to external categorizations. 
However, the study also observes the characters’ agency in negotiating their identities and challenging 
societal expectations. The study explores identity dynamics, emphasizing the importance of social and 
contextual factors in shaping individual and collective identities, particularly within historical and 
cultural narratives.
Key Words: Identity, Belonging, Social Identity Theory, Partition of India, Categorization, 
Social Comparison, Identification, Khushwant Singh, Train to Pakistan
Corresponding Author: Muhammad Hamzah Masood (Lecturer, Department of Linguistics and 
Communications, University of Management and Technology, Lahore, Punjab, Pakistan. 
Email: hamzah.masood@umt.edu.pk) 
Introduction 
The Partition of India in 1947 marked a 
significant period of upheaval, violence, and 
displacement, during which the concepts of 
identity and belonging took on new 
dimensions (Mehta & Mookerjea-Leonard, 
2014). Khushwant Singh’s novel, Train to 
Pakistan, offers a compelling narrative set
2
Exploring Identity and Belonging in the Context of Partition of 1947: A Social Identity Theory Analysis of 
‘Train to Pakistan’ by Khushwant Singh 
Vol. VIII, No. I (Winter 2023)
Page | 205 
against this backdrop, providing a rich 
context for exploring identity and belonging. 
This research paper applies Social Identity 
Theory to examine how the characters in the 
novel navigate their sense of self and 
belonging 
within 
the 
socio-political 
dynamics of the Partition. 
This research paper applies the Social 
Identity Theory framework to examine the 
theme 
of 
identity 
and 
belonging 
in 
Khushwant Singh’s (2016) novel, Train to 
Pakistan. The study seeks to understand how 
individuals in the novel develop a sense of 
identity and belonging within the socio-
political context of the Partition of India in 
1947. The research methodology involves a 
qualitative analysis of the novel, drawing on 
close reading and thematic analysis to 
identify 
instances 
and 
dialogues 
that 
highlight the characters’ experiences related 
to identity and belonging. 
The Social Identity Theory theoretical 
framework, developed by Henri Tajfel and 
John Turner, explores the processes of 
categorization, 
social 
comparison, 
and 
identification within the novel (Tajfel & 
Turner, 2004). The study investigates how 
the characters categorize themselves and 
others based on religious and cultural 
affiliations, and how these categories shape 
their understanding of identity. Social 
comparison is examined to understand how 
characters compare their own religious group 
with others, leading to in-group favouritism 
and out-group discrimination. Furthermore, 
the study explores the identification process 
as characters align themselves with their 
religious and cultural groups, emphasizing 
the significance of shared norms, values, and 
customs. 
The research findings reveal that their 
religious, cultural, and national affiliations 
deeply influence the characters’ sense of 
identity 
and 
belonging. 
The 
analysis 
highlights 
the 
complexity 
of 
identity 
construction during conflict and how it 
shapes individuals’ behaviours and attitudes 
(Mavor et al., 2017). It is observed that the 
characters’ identities are subject to external 
categorizations, often leading to stereotypes 
and prejudice. However, the characters also 
exhibit agency in negotiating their identities 
and challenging societal expectations (Tajfel, 
2010). 
The 
research 
contributes 
to 
understanding identity dynamics within the 
context of the Partition and provides insights 
into the human experience of identity and 
belonging. The study underscores the 
relevance of Social Identity Theory in 
analyzing historical and cultural narratives, 
emphasizing the importance of social and 
contextual factors in shaping individual and 
collective 
identities. 
This 
research 
contributes to the existing literature on 
identity, belonging, and the impact of socio-
political events on individual and collective 
consciousness.
Statement of the Problem 
The Partition of India in 1947 significantly 
disrupted millions of individuals’ identities 
and sense of belonging (Ambedkar, 2023). 
Khushwant Singh’s (2016) Train to Pakistan 
provides a narrative that portrays the 
experiences 
of 
characters 
during 
this 
tumultuous period. However, there is a need 
to explore further and understand how 
individuals in the novel develop their 
identities and negotiate their sense of 
belonging within the socio-political context 
of the Partition. This research aims to address 
this 
gap 
by 
applying 
the 
theoretical 
framework of Social Identity Theory to 
analyze the processes of categorization, 
social comparison, and identification in the 
formation of identity and belonging in the 
novel. By delving into these themes, this 
study seeks to provide insights into the 
complex dynamics of identity and belonging 
during times of conflict and to contribute to 
the existing literature on identity, belonging, 
and the impact of socio-political events on 
individual and collective consciousness.
3
Bushra Saeed, Shahzeb Shafi and Muhammad Hamzah Masood 
Page | 206
Global Language Review (GLR) 
Research Questions 
The current study answers the following 
research questions: 
1. 
How do the characters in Train to 
Pakistan by Khushwant Singh develop 
their sense of identity within the socio-
political context of the Partition? 
2. How do the characters in the novel 
negotiate their sense of belonging 
amidst the religious, cultural, and 
national 
affiliations 
during 
the 
Partition? 
3. To what extent does Social Identity 
Theory help understand the processes 
of categorization, social comparison, 
and identification in forming identity 
and belonging in the novel? 
Research Objectives 
The objective of the present study is as 
follows: 
1. 
To examine the characters’ experiences 
of identity and belonging in Train to 
Pakistan within the context of the 
Partition 
2. To 
explore 
the 
processes 
of 
categorization, social comparison, and 
identification depicted in the novel, 
and their influence on the characters’ 
sense of identity and belonging 
3. To apply Social Identity Theory as a 
framework for analyzing the dynamics 
of identity and belonging in the novel
Significance of the Study 
This 
research 
aims 
to 
contribute 
to 
understanding identity and belonging within 
the context of the Partition, utilizing the lens 
of Social Identity Theory. The analysis sheds 
light on the characters’ experiences and 
provides insights into the complex dynamics 
of identity formation and negotiation during 
the conflict. The findings of this research may 
inform broader discussions on the impact of 
socio-political events on individual and 
collective identities, fostering a deeper 
understanding of the human experience of 
identity and belonging.
Limitations 
It is important to acknowledge certain 
limitations of this research. Firstly, the 
analysis is limited to the data presented in 
the novel Train to Pakistan, which may not 
fully capture the breadth and complexity of 
identity and belonging during the Partition. 
Additionally, the researcher’s own biases and 
interpretations may influence the analysis. 
However, steps were taken to minimize these 
limitations through reflexivity, transparency, 
and consultation with experts.
Literature Review 
The literature review provides an overview of 
previous studies on identity, belonging, and 
the Partition. It explores the theoretical 
underpinnings of Social Identity Theory and 
its 
relevance 
to 
understanding 
the 
complexities of identity formation and 
belonging in times of conflict. Additionally, it 
highlights key concepts and constructs 
within Social Identity Theory for the analysis 
of Train to Pakistan.
